category_title: Students who talk out to access peer attention
minimal_context: Craig is in the general classroom during independent work time, expected to complete his assigned tasks.
dialogue:
TUTOR: It is time for independent work. Please start working quietly on your assigned tasks.

STUDENT: (Turns to a peer) Hey, did you see the game last night?

TUTOR: Craig, remember the rules for independent work time.

STUDENT: I am just asking a question. (Turns back to peer) Seriously though, what did you think of that last play?

TUTOR: Please focus on your own assignment.

STUDENT: (Laughs as peer responds back) I know, right? It was crazy! Look at this drawing I did of it.

options.A: Implement independent work activities with less demanding rules and expectations for Craig during his assigned tasks in the general classroom setting.
options.B: Provide Craig's assigned tasks using large fonts with supporting pictures, reductions in the number of questions, access to word banks, and speech-generating devices.
options.C: Engage Craig in alternative tasks such as drawing, painting, water coloring, and pottery making during independent work time in the general classroom.
options.D: Teach Craig to use business-size appointment cards with a checklist of choices to access peer attention during designated times, and honor these scheduled meetings.
